item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image was created by the user.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is awesome. 1+

Congratulations!

Also, clever twist on the eternal conflict between pro-life and pro-choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it. Thanks for the heads up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum isn't showing up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you DID there.

I really liked this, but I'm really surprised some people missed the "It uses the souls of unborn babies" part. I thought it was obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, even though I completely missed the subtext before I read the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a set of clothes that made you believe it could prevent pregnancy

I think it says clearly that (i) it does really prevent pregnancy and (ii) it does not induce people to believe in it's effect. It works regardless of what the user believes, but the only people who will be affected are the ones gullible enough to believe it and use it exclusively (although gullible may not be the right word, because it does work as advertised)

It's a bit like a The Factory product in that respect; people are not compelled to use it, they just do because it is convenient. It's true the souls could be made more explicit, but then I feel it would lose it's charm, so I will leave it as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must admit I didn't really get the hook here until I read the comments. I completely missed the implications of the testimonial (namely, that it recycles the souls of the unborn) and just assumed it was a set of clothes that made you believe it could prevent pregnancy and also made you insane for no apparent reason. Still not entirely convinced by this - no vote for now, but happy to upvote if the conceit is clarified a little more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice :) Glad I could help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:) It could be that it was just obvious to you.

Basically, the devices work by capturing the newborn soul of the just-conceived child and grafting it to the wearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what's supposed to be implied in the article? I pretty much took the whole thing at face value, so I guess I must have not gotten some part of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Innnteresting. I remember seeing this on the forums and wondering how the heck that concept would ever work, but I really like your execution. I actually had to read it a few times before the implication hit. Nicely done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I dunno how much help I was with this but you're welcome nonetheless. I liked this is in the drafts and I like it here.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I changed ''did not develop personality…'' to ''do not develop DID''. Since I'm defining the abbreviation I might as well use it. I think you're right that's it's more accurate like that. Just personnality disorder could mean a lot of things apart from DID.

When I started the draft I was going to write ''multiple personality disorder'' then I realised DID was the current terminology and made for a cool logo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm just being pedantic

Well, yes and no.

Dissociative Identity Disorder could be called a personality disorder in that it is characterized by several distinct personalities existing within one skull. However, it's not necessarily a personality disorder in the same way that histrionic personality, antisocial personality, and borderline personality disorder are.

In the minds of some psychologists/psychiatrists, you're probably right. But the colloquial usage is probably sufficient here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I didn't say personality vs identity. I said a personality disorder verses a dissociative disorder. DID as far as I understood was a dissociative disorder, not a personality disorder. Therefore saying that a person didn't develop a personality disorder is kind of off-the-mark, when you are referring to any kind of dissociative disorder.

(I mean, I'm not an expert either, but it kind of bugs me. Maybe I'm just being pedantic, I apologize.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, too, had been expecting some sort of anti-Nike anart or somesuch. This is much better than I anticipated, and the implications are boggling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, the advertisment in total just ruined it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Just Do It!"

"Just DID!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's because they're produced by very evil entities. The Office québécois de la langue française was very angry about those fliers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it's from Montreal, it needs proper bilingual advertising.

</fussbudget>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The terms personnality and identity are sort of use interchangeably. I'm not an expert myself, but from Wikipedia:

Dissociative identity disorder (DID), previously known as multiple personality disorder (MPD),[1] is a mental disorder characterized by at least two distinct and relatively enduring identities or dissociated personality states…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was heading towards the upvote, but the last bit just killed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
found to develop complex dissociative identity disorder
did not develop any personality disorder

I don't believe DID is considered a personality disorder, it's considered a dissociative disorder? Maybe I simply don't understand the way these terms and placed.

I don't like this SCP because the idea doesn't grab me very well- I understand its subtleties but I can't find myself enjoying any part of this skip. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, okay. Indeed, they're quite different, and I can definitely see where you're coming from with the Being John Malkovitch thing. A movie that was far more grim and cynical than I had expected it to be. Seriously, I think Kaufmann has a thing against having remotely sympathetic characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was not originally inspired by it, but when I bounced the idea in the forum someone mentioned it, so I made sure that it was significantly different. If I can think of an inspiration it would be the movie Being John Malkovitch. And mental illness are both very interesting and scary to me. I did not notice the similarity in number however. All my SCP so far start with 18 and I almost chose 1821.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good.

Also, now I'm just waiting for SCiPs with main list names like "Get a load of this!" and "Time for a change of pace!" XD

PS: I apologize for that reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cleverly done, and I like very much how the ad isn't even misleading, it's just that no one unfamiliar with the supernatural would guess (or believe) from this just what it actually does.

Seems vaguely reminiscent of my SCP-1088, in that they're both about the souls of potential babies finding a way back into the world. Actually, the numbers are similar enough, I have to ask. Was 1088 partial inspiration for this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent subtle suggestion. I can't think of a better way to have put this together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. Very subtle. The fact that the Foundation avoids acknowledging the existence of souls was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does a very good job of implying things without actually stating them. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Doctor Anborough, Scantron & DrBerggren for their helpful comments. (I improved my Paint logo a bit but I might create a higher-quality picture in Illustrator later if this one survives.)
